works.title: The Language of Flowers
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Language of Flowers" is an unpublished song from a poem by the American geologist and poet James Gates Percival, with music written by the English composer Edward Elgar when he was only fourteen years old.

works.title: Through the Long Days
works.wikipedia_extract: "Through the Long Days" is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1885 as No. 2 of his Op. 16, Three Songs. The words are from a poem by the American writer and statesman John Hay.

works.title: Is she not passing fair?
works.wikipedia_extract: "Is she not passing fair?" is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar.

works.title: As I laye a-thynkynge
works.wikipedia_extract: "As I laye a-thynkynge" is the last poem written by "Thomas Ingoldsby". It was set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar.

works.title: A Song of Autumn
works.wikipedia_extract: "A Song of Autumn" is a poem by Adam Lindsay Gordon set to music by Edward Elgar in 1892.

works.title: Sursum Corda
works.wikipedia_extract: Sursum corda, Op. 11 is a musical work by the English composer Edward Elgar for strings, brass, timpani and organ, composed in 1894. The composer dedicated it to his friend Henry Dyke Acland (1850-1936), an amateur cellist who was his golfing companion, manager of the Worcester Old Bank in Malvern, and son of Henry Acland.

works.title: A Song of Flight
works.wikipedia_extract: "A Song of Flight" is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1895, as his Op. 31, No. 2, with the words from a poem by Christina Rossetti.

works.title: After
works.wikipedia_extract: "After" is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1895, as his Op. 31, No. 1, with the words from a poem by Philip Bourke Marston.

works.title: From the Bavarian Highlands
works.wikipedia_extract: From the Bavarian Highlands, Op 27 is a work for choir and orchestra by Edward Elgar.

works.title: Love alone will stay
works.wikipedia_extract: "Love alone will stay" is a poem by Caroline Alice Elgar, set to music for voice and piano by her husband, the English composer Edward Elgar, in 1897.

works.title: Dry those fair, those crystal eyes
works.wikipedia_extract: "Dry those fair, those chrystal eyes" is a sonnet by Henry King (1591-1669), Bishop of Chichester.

works.title: Sea Pictures
works.wikipedia_extract:
Sea Pictures, Op. 37 is a song cycle for contralto and orchestra by Edward Elgar. It consists of settings of a poems by five different authors. It also exists in a version for solo voice with piano accompaniment.
The songs are:"Sea Slumber Song" by Roden Noel
"In Haven (Capri)" by Alice Elgar, the composer's wife
"Sabbath Morning at Sea" by Elizabeth Barrett Browning
"Where Corals Lie" by Richard Garnett
"The Swimmer" by Adam Lindsay Gordon

works.title: Always and Everywhere
works.wikipedia_extract: "Always and Everywhere" is a song by the English composer Edward Elgar with words translated from the Polish of Zygmunt Krasiński's poem "Zawsze i wszędzie" by Frank H. Fortey. It was composed and published in 1901.

works.title: Come, Gentle Night!
works.wikipedia_extract: "Come, Gentle Night!" is a poem by Clifton Bingham set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1901.

works.title: Incidental Music and Funeral March from Grania and Diarmid
Speak, Music!
works.wikipedia_extract: Speak, Music! is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1901 as his Op.41, No.2.

works.title: The Kingdom
works.wikipedia_extract: The Kingdom, Op. 51, is an English-language oratorio by Edward Elgar. It is written for four soloists – soprano, contralto, tenor and bass – choir and large orchestra. The work was first performed at the Birmingham Triennial Music Festival on 3 October 1906. It continues the narrative of the lives of Christ's disciples begun in his earlier oratorio, The Apostles. It depicts the community of the early church, Pentecost, and the events of the next few days.

works.title: Speak, my Heart!
works.wikipedia_extract: "Speak, my Heart" is a poem by Arthur Christopher Benson, set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1902.

works.title: Introduction and Allegro
works.wikipedia_extract: Edward Elgar's Introduction and Allegro for Strings, Op. 47, was composed in 1905 for performance in an all-Elgar concert by the newly formed London Symphony Orchestra. It is a string concertante piece scored for string quartet and string orchestra, Elgar wrote it to show off the players' virtuosity. For its structure he drew on the 18th-century concerto grosso form.

works.title: Pleading
works.wikipedia_extract: "Pleading" is a poem written by Arthur L. Salmon, and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1908, as his Op.48.

works.title: Oh, soft was the song
works.wikipedia_extract: Oh, soft was the song is a song with words by Gilbert Parker set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1910, as his Op. 59, No. 3. It is the second and last verse of a poem At Sea which Parker published in Volume I of a series of poems called Embers. The Opus 59 songs were part of a song-cycle of six romantic songs by Parker that was never completed – Nos 1, 2 and 4 were never composed. The other songs were Was it some Golden Star? and Twilight. The songs were originally written with piano accompaniment, but this was later re-scored by the composer for full orchestra.

works.title: Twilight
works.wikipedia_extract:
Twilight is a song with music by the English composer Edward Elgar written in 1910 as his Op. 59, No. 6. Elgar set the words of the poem The Twilight of Love, from Volume 2 of a series of poems called Embers by Sir Gilbert Parker.
The Opus 59 songs were part of a song-cycle of six romantic songs by Parker that was never completed – Nos 1, 2 and 4 were never composed. The other songs were Oh, soft was the song and Was it some Golden Star?. The songs were originally written with piano accompaniment, but this was later re-scored by the composer for full orchestra.

works.title: The Music Makers
works.wikipedia_extract: The Music Makers, Op. 69, is a work for contralto or mezzo-soprano, chorus and orchestra composed by Edward Elgar. It was first performed at the Birmingham Festival in 1912.

works.title: Arabian Serenade
works.wikipedia_extract: "Arabian Serenade" is a poem written by Margery Lawrence and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1914.

works.title: Sospiri
works.wikipedia_extract: Sospiri, Op. 70, is an adagio for string orchestra, harp, and organ composed by Edward Elgar just before and performed just after the beginning of World War I.

works.title: The Chariots of the Lord
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Chariots of the Lord" is a poem by Rev. John Brownlie, D.D., set to music by Edward Elgar in 1914.

works.title: Fight for Right
works.wikipedia_extract: "Fight for Right" is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar, with words taken from the epic poem The Story of Sigurd the Volsung by William Morris.

works.title: Violin Sonata
works.wikipedia_extract: Sir Edward Elgar wrote his Violin Sonata in E minor, Op. 82, in 1918, at the same time as he wrote his String Quartet in E minor and his Piano Quintet in A minor. These three chamber music works were all written at "Brinkwells", the country house near Fittleworth in West Sussex that Lady Elgar had acquired for her husband to recuperate and compose in, and they mark his major contribution to the chamber music genre. His Cello Concerto in E minor of 1919 completed the quartet of introspective and melancholy works that comprised Elgar's last major creative spurt before his death in 1934.

works.title: XTC
works.wikipedia_extract: "XTC" ("Ecstasy") is a song with words and music written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1930. It was his last song, and written for the soprano Joan Elwes.

works.title: A Child Asleep
works.wikipedia_extract: "A Child Asleep" is a song, with lyrics from a poem written by Elizabeth Barrett Browning. It was set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in December 1909 and published in 1910 by Novello. It was first published by Browning in 1840.

works.title: A Song of Union
works.wikipedia_extract: "A Song of Union" is a poem written by Alfred Noyes, and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar. It was one of the songs written to be performed in the Pageant of Empire at the British Empire Exhibition on 21 July 1924, though this particular song was not performed at the Pageant.

works.title: A War Song
works.wikipedia_extract: "A War Song", originally called "A Soldier's Song", was a poem written by C. Flavell Hayward and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1884.

works.title: Big Steamers
works.wikipedia_extract: "Big Steamers" is a poem by Rudyard Kipling, first published in 1911 as one of his twenty-three poems written specially for C. R. L. Fletcher's "A School History of England". It appears in the last chapter of the book. It is intended for children, with the verses responding with facts and humour to their curiosity about the 'big steamers' - as the merchant ships are called.

works.title: Canto Popolare
works.wikipedia_extract: In the South (Alassio), Op. 50, is a concert overture composed by Edward Elgar during a family holiday in Italy in the winter of 1903 to 1904. He was working on a symphony, but the local atmosphere inspired him instead to write what some have seen as a tone poem, with an Italian flavour. At about 20 minutes' duration it was the composer's longest sustained orchestral piece to that time.

works.title: Carillon
works.wikipedia_extract: Carillon is a recitation with orchestral accompaniment written by the English composer Edward Elgar as his Op. 75, in 1914. The words are by the Belgian poet Émile Cammaerts.

works.title: Carissima
works.wikipedia_extract: "Carissima" is a piece for small orchestra by the English composer Sir Edward Elgar.

works.title: Chanson de Matin
works.wikipedia_extract: Chanson de Matin, Op. 15, No. 2, is a musical work composed by Edward Elgar for violin and piano, and later orchestrated by the composer. Its first publication was in 1899, though it is thought that it was almost certainly written in 1889 or 1890.

works.title: Chanson de Nuit
works.wikipedia_extract: Chanson de Nuit, Op. 15, No. 1, is a musical work composed by Edward Elgar for violin and piano, and later orchestrated by the composer. Its first publication was in 1897, though it is considered that it was almost certainly written in 1889 or 1890.

works.title: Cockaigne
works.wikipedia_extract: Cockaigne (In London Town), Op. 40, also known as the Cockaigne Overture, is a concert overture for full orchestra written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1900–1901. It was his first purely orchestral work after his Enigma Variations had propelled him to national celebrity and it was an immediate success and has been much performed in concert and on record ever since.

works.title: Concert Allegro
works.wikipedia_extract: The Concert Allegro, Op. 46 by Sir Edward Elgar is a piece of music for solo piano. It takes about 10 minutes to perform. It is the only piano work he wrote that was designed for concert performance. It is in the key of C major and was written in 1901, at the request of the pianist Fanny Davies.

works.title: Coronation Ode
works.wikipedia_extract: Coronation Ode, Op. 44 is a work composed by Edward Elgar for soprano, alto, tenor and bass soloists, chorus and orchestra, with words by A. C. Benson.

works.title: Diarmuid and Grania
works.wikipedia_extract: Diarmuid and Grania is a play in poetic prose co-written by George Moore and W. B. Yeats in 1901, with incidental music by the English composer Edward Elgar.

works.title: Dream Children
works.wikipedia_extract:
Dream Children, Op 43 is a musical work for small orchestra by Edward Elgar. There are two movements:1. Andante in G minor
2. Allegretto piacevole in G major

works.title: Duett for trombone and double bass
works.wikipedia_extract: Duett for trombone and double bass is a musical work by the English composer Edward Elgar.

works.title: Falstaff
works.wikipedia_extract: Falstaff – Symphonic Study in C minor, Op. 68, is an orchestral work by the English composer Edward Elgar. Though not so designated by the composer, it is a symphonic poem in the tradition of Franz Liszt and Richard Strauss. It portrays Sir John Falstaff, the "fat knight" of William Shakespeare's Henry IV Parts 1 and 2.

works.title: Follow the Colours
works.wikipedia_extract: "Follow the Colours" is a marching song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1907, with words by Capt. William de Courcy Stretton. The song is for male voice solo with an optional male voice chorus, accompanied by piano, orchestra or military band.

works.title: Froissart Overture
works.wikipedia_extract: Froissart, Op. 19, is a concert overture by Edward Elgar, inspired by the 14th-century Chronicles of Jean Froissart. Elgar was first attracted to the Chronicles after finding mention of them in Walter Scott's Old Mortality.

works.title: Good Morrow
works.wikipedia_extract: "Good Morrowe" is a poem written by George Gascoigne in 1557 and set to music by the English composer Sir Edward Elgar in 1929. Elgar titled it in modern English "Good Morrow" with the subtitle "A simple Carol for His Majesty's happy recovery", and it is a setting for unaccompanied four-part choir (SATB), though a piano accompaniment is provided.

works.title: Idylle
works.wikipedia_extract: Idylle is a piece for violin and piano composed by Edward Elgar in 1884, as his Opus 4, No. 1. Appended to the title is the description Esquisse Facile, which means "Easy Sketch". It was Elgar's first published work.

works.title: Imperial March
works.wikipedia_extract: Imperial March is a piece for full orchestra written by the English composer Edward Elgar to celebrate the Diamond Jubilee of Queen Victoria in 1897, as his Op. 32.

works.title: In Haven
works.wikipedia_extract: "In Haven (Capri)" is a poem by Caroline Alice Elgar, probably best known in its musical setting as the second (and shortest) song composed by her husband, Edward Elgar, for his song-cycle Sea Pictures.

works.title: In Moonlight
works.wikipedia_extract: "In Moonlight" is a song with music written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1904 to words from the poem "An Ariette for Music. To a Lady singing to her Accompaniment on the Guitar", by Percy Bysshe Shelley (1792–1822) and published in 1832.

works.title: In the Dawn
works.wikipedia_extract: In the Dawn is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1901 as his Op.41, No.1.

works.title: In the South
works.wikipedia_extract: In the South (Alassio), Op. 50, is a concert overture composed by Edward Elgar during a family holiday in Italy in the winter of 1903 to 1904. He was working on a symphony, but the local atmosphere inspired him instead to write what some have seen as a tone poem, with an Italian flavour. At about 20 minutes' duration it was the composer's longest sustained orchestral piece to that time.

works.title: Inside the Bar
works.wikipedia_extract: ”Inside the Bar" is a song written in 1917 by the English composer Edward Elgar, with words by Sir Gilbert Parker.

works.title: It isnae me
works.wikipedia_extract: "It isnae me" is a poem by Sally Holmes which was set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1930.

works.title: La Capricieuse
works.wikipedia_extract: La Capricieuse Op.17, is a showpiece by the English composer Edward Elgar written in 1891 for solo violin and piano accompaniment. It has been recorded by notable violinists including Jascha Heifetz, Ivry Gitlis, and Itzhak Perlman, among others.

works.title: Land of Hope and Glory
works.wikipedia_extract: "Land of Hope and Glory" is a British patriotic song, with music by Edward Elgar, written in 1901 and with lyrics by A. C. Benson added in 1902.

works.title: Le drapeau belge
works.wikipedia_extract: Le drapeau belge is a recitation with orchestral accompaniment written by the English composer Edward Elgar as his Op. 79, in 1917. The words are by the Belgian poet Émile Cammaerts. The poem reflects on the wartime meaning of the colours of the Belgian flag.

works.title: Like to the Damask Rose
works.wikipedia_extract: ”Like to the Damask Rose” is a poem either by Francis Quarles called "Hos ego versiculos", or by Simon Wastell called “The flesh profiteth nothing”. It was set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1892.

works.title: Merchant Adventurers
works.wikipedia_extract: "Merchant Adventurers" is a poem written by Alfred Noyes, and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar. It was one of the songs written to be performed in the Pageant of Empire at the British Empire Exhibition on 21 July 1924.

works.title: Nursery Suite
works.wikipedia_extract: The Nursery Suite is one of the last compositions by Edward Elgar. Like Elgar's The Wand of Youth suites, it makes use of sketches from the composer's childhood.

works.title: Organ Sonata
works.wikipedia_extract: The Sonata in G major, Op. 28 is Edward Elgar's only sonata composed for the organ and was first performed on 8 July 1895. It also exists in an arrangement for full orchestra made after Elgar's death.

works.title: Overture in D minor
works.wikipedia_extract: The Overture in D minor  is a transcription made in 1923 by Sir Edward Elgar of a musical work by George Frideric Handel composed in 1717-18.

works.title: Pageant of Empire
works.wikipedia_extract: Pageant of Empire is the title given to a set of songs, to words by Alfred Noyes, written by the English composer Sir Edward Elgar and given important positions in the Pageant of Empire at the British Empire Exhibition at Wembley Park.

works.title: Piano Quintet
works.wikipedia_extract: The Quintet in A minor for Piano and String Quartet, Op. 84 is a chamber work by Edward Elgar.

works.title: Polonia
works.wikipedia_extract: Polonia is a symphonic prelude by the English composer Edward Elgar written in 1915 as his Op. 76.

works.title: Pomp and Circumstance March in D Major, Op. 39, No. 1
Powick Asylum Music
works.wikipedia_extract: The Powick Asylum Music consists of a number of sets of dance music – quadrilles and polkas – written by Edward Elgar during his time as bandmaster at the Worcester City and County Lunatic Asylum between 1879 and 1884. The music was not published, but the original manuscripts of instrumental parts are preserved in the collection of the Elgar Birthplace Museum.

works.title: Quand nos bourgeons se rouvriront
works.wikipedia_extract: "Quand nos bourgeons se rouvriront" ("When the spring comes round") is a song for solo soprano, part of a staged recitation with orchestra Une voix dans le désert written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1915. The words are by the Belgian poet Émile Cammaerts.

works.title: Queen Alexandra's Memorial Ode
works.wikipedia_extract: Queen Alexandra's Memorial Ode, otherwise known as "So many true Princesses who have gone", is an ode written by John Masefield and set to music for choir and orchestra by Sir Edward Elgar for the occasion of the unveiling of Sir Alfred Gilbert's memorial to Queen Alexandra on 8 June 1932 outside Marlborough House in London.

works.title: Queen Mary's Song
works.wikipedia_extract: "Queen Mary's Song" is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1889. The words are by Tennyson, sung by Queen Mary I of England as she plays a lute in scene 2, act 5 of his 1875 play Queen Mary: A Drama.

works.title: Romance for bassoon
works.wikipedia_extract: The Romance, in D minor, Op 62, is a short work for bassoon and orchestra by Edward Elgar. It exists also in a transcription for cello and orchestra made by the composer. Both the bassoon and cello versions date from 1909–10. It is also published with the orchestral part reduced to a piano accompaniment.

works.title: Romance for violin
works.wikipedia_extract: The Romance in E minor for violin and piano is a work by Edward Elgar composed in 1878 or 1879 and published in 1885 as his Opus 1.

works.title: Rondel
works.wikipedia_extract: "Rondel" is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1894 as his Op. 16, No. 3. The words are by Longfellow, a translation of a Rondel by Froissart. The manuscript is dated 4 January 1894.

works.title: Roundel: The little eyes that never knew Light
works.wikipedia_extract: "Roundel: The little eyes that never knew Light" is a song with piano accompaniment written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1897. The words are from the fourth roundel of a poem A Baby's Death written by A. C. Swinburne and originally published in the book A Century of Roundels.

works.title: Sabbath Morning at Sea
works.wikipedia_extract: "Sabbath Morning at Sea" is a poem by Elizabeth Barrett Browning first published in 1839, which Edward Elgar set to music in 1899 as the third song in his song-cycle Sea Pictures.

works.title: Sailing Westward
works.wikipedia_extract: "Sailing Westward" is a poem written by Alfred Noyes, and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar. It was one of the songs written to be performed in the Pageant of Empire at the British Empire Exhibition, Wembley Park, on 21 July 1924.

works.title: Scenes from the Saga of King Olaf
works.wikipedia_extract: King Olaf is a cantata by the British composer Edward Elgar, scored for soloists, chorus and orchestra. It was commissioned for the North Staffordshire Music Festival of 1896, where it was well received. It went on to be performed by choral societies in other parts of the country.

works.title: Sea Slumber Song
works.wikipedia_extract: "Sea Slumber Song" is a 19th-century poem by Roden Noel set to music by Edward Elgar as the first song in his song-cycle Sea Pictures (1899).

works.title: Serenade for Strings
works.wikipedia_extract: The Serenade for String Orchestra in E minor, Op. 20, is an early piece in three short movements, by Edward Elgar. It was written in March 1892 as a present to his wife, Alice on the occasion of their third wedding anniversary that year and first performed privately in that year; its public premiere was in 1896. It became one of Elgar's most popular compositions, and has been recorded many times.

works.title: Seven Lieder of Edward Elgar
works.wikipedia_extract: Seven Lieder is a set of songs by the English composer Edward Elgar published together in 1907, by Ascherberg, Hopwood & Crew Ltd.

works.title: Sevillana
works.wikipedia_extract: Sevillana, or, as the composer titled it Sevillaña , is a short piece for orchestra by the English composer Edward Elgar written in 1884 and published as his Op. 7. It was first published by Tuckwood, with the composer's revision of 1889 published by Ascherberg in 1895. It was dedicated to W. C. Stockley, conductor of the Birmingham Festival.

works.title: Shakespeare's Kingdom
works.wikipedia_extract: "Shakespeare's Kingdom" is a poem written by Alfred Noyes and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar. It was one of the songs written to be performed in the Pageant of Empire at the British Empire Exhibition on 21 July 1924.

works.title: String Quartet
works.wikipedia_extract: The String Quartet in E minor, Op. 83, was one of three major chamber music works composed by Sir Edward Elgar in 1918. The others were the Violin Sonata in E minor, Op. 82, and the Piano Quintet in A minor, Op. 84. Along with the Cello Concerto in E minor, Op. 85 of 1919, these were to be his last major completed works prior to his death in 1934.

works.title: Symphony No. 1
works.wikipedia_extract: Sir Edward Elgar's Symphony No. 1 in A♭ major, Op. 55 is one of his two completed symphonies. The first performance was given by the Hallé Orchestra conducted by Hans Richter in Manchester, England, on 3 December 1908. It was widely known that Elgar had been planning a symphony for more than ten years, and the announcement that he had finally completed it aroused enormous interest. The critical reception was enthusiastic, and the public response unprecedented. The symphony achieved what The Musical Times described as "immediate and phenomenal success", with a hundred performances in Britain, continental Europe and America within just over a year of its première.

works.title: Symphony No. 2
works.wikipedia_extract: Sir Edward Elgar's Symphony No. 2 in E♭ major, Op. 63, was completed on 28 February 1911 and was premiered at the London Musical Festival at the Queen's Hall by the Queen's Hall Orchestra on 24 May 1911 with the composer conducting. The work, which Elgar called "the passionate pilgrimage of the soul", was his last completed symphony; the composition of his Symphony No. 3, begun in 1933, was cut short by his death in 1934.

works.title: Symphony No. 3
works.wikipedia_extract: Edward Elgar's Third Symphony Op. 88 (posth.) was incomplete at the time of his death in 1934. Elgar left 130 pages of sketches, which the British composer Anthony Payne worked on for many years, producing a complete symphony in 1997, officially known as "Edward Elgar: the sketches for Symphony No 3 elaborated by Anthony Payne" or in brief "Elgar/Payne Symphony No 3". The first public performance was at the Royal Festival Hall, London, on 15 February 1998, by the BBC Symphony Orchestra conducted by Andrew Davis.

works.title: The Apostles
works.wikipedia_extract: The Apostles, Op. 49, is an oratorio for soloists, chorus and orchestra composed by Edward Elgar. It was first performed on 14 October 1903 at the Birmingham Music Festival.

works.title: The Black Knight
works.wikipedia_extract: The Black Knight, Op. 25 is a symphony/cantata for orchestra and chorus written by Edward Elgar in 1889–93. The librettist borrows from Longfellow's translation of the ballad Der schwarze Ritter by Ludwig Uhland.

works.title: The Blue Mountains
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Blue Mountains" is a song by the English composer Edward Elgar, with lyrics from a poem written by Alfred Noyes. It was one of the songs written to be performed in the Pageant of Empire at the British Empire Exhibition in Wembley Park, London, on 21 July 1924.

works.title: The Bottomless Bag
works.wikipedia_extract: The Bottomless Bag is a 2017 Russian historical drama film directed by Rustam Khamdamov. The film is based on Ryunosuke Akutagawa's 1922 story In a Grove which takes place during the times of Tsar Alexander II.

works.title: The Crown of India
works.wikipedia_extract: The Crown of India, was a masque, an elaborate theatrical presentation, staged in 1912 to celebrate the visit the preceding December of King George V and Queen Mary to Delhi for their coronation as Emperor and Empress of India. For this masque, the English composer Sir Edward Elgar wrote the music as his Op. 66, with a libretto by Henry Hamilton. The masque consisted of two tableaux: "The Cities of Ind" and "Ave Imperator!".

works.title: The Dream of Gerontius
works.wikipedia_extract: The Dream of Gerontius, Op. 38, is a work for voices and orchestra in two parts composed by Edward Elgar in 1900, to text from the poem by John Henry Newman. It relates the journey of a pious man's soul from his deathbed to his judgment before God and settling into Purgatory. Elgar disapproved of the use of the term oratorio for the work, though his wishes are not always followed. The piece is widely regarded as Elgar's finest choral work, and some consider it his masterpiece.

works.title: The Globalist
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Globalist" is a song by English rock band Muse, and the eleventh track from the band's seventh studio album, Drones. An apocalyptic song, it serves as a sequel to the song "Citizen Erased" from their 2001 album Origin of Symmetry. Part of this song contains music based on "Nimrod" from Enigma Variations, composed by Edward Elgar. The song follows "Aftermath", which centers on the album's protagonist discovering love. Matt Bellamy has said that the album's negative stories, "The Globalist" and "Drones", together serve as an epilogue. At ten minutes and seven seconds, it is the second longest song in Muse's discography, after "Exogenesis: Symphony".

works.title: The Heart of Canada
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Heart of Canada" is a poem written by Alfred Noyes, and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar. It was one of the songs written to be performed in the Pageant of Empire at the British Empire Exhibition at Wembley Park on 21 July 1924.

works.title: The Immortal Legions
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Immortal Legions" is a poem written by Alfred Noyes, and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar. It was one of the songs written to be performed in the Pageant of Empire at the British Empire Exhibition on 21 July 1924.

works.title: The Islands
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Islands" is a poem written by Alfred Noyes, and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar. It was one of the songs written to be performed in the Pageant of Empire at the British Empire Exhibition on 21 July 1924.

works.title: The King's Way
works.wikipedia_extract: Kingsway or King's Way may refer to:

works.title: The Lowestoft Boat
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Lowestoft Boat" is a poem written by Rudyard Kipling, and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1917, as the first of a set of four war-related songs on nautical subjects for which he chose the title "The Fringes of the Fleet".

works.title: The Pipes of Pan
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Pipes of Pan" is a poem by Adrian Ross set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar, being completed on 5 June 1899.

works.title: The Poet's Life
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Poet's Life" is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1892, with words by "Ellen Burroughs".

works.title: The River
works.wikipedia_extract: The River is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1909 as his Op.60, No.2.

works.title: The Sanguine Fan
works.wikipedia_extract: The Sanguine Fan, Op. 81, is a single-act ballet written by Edward Elgar in 1917. It was composed to raise money for wartime charities, and after two performances in 1917 and a recording of excerpts in 1920, the score was neglected until 1973, when the conductor Sir Adrian Boult revived it for a recording. It was later staged in the theatre by the London Festival Ballet.

works.title: The Severn Suite
works.wikipedia_extract: The Severn Suite, Opus 87, is a musical work written by Sir Edward Elgar. It is a late composition, written in 1930, the result of an invitation to write a test piece for the National Brass Band Championship. It was dedicated to his friend, the author and critic George Bernard Shaw.

works.title: The Spirit of England
works.wikipedia_extract: The Spirit of England, Op. 80, is a work for chorus, orchestra, and soprano/tenor soloist in three movements composed by Edward Elgar between 1915 and 1917, setting text from Laurence Binyon's 1914 anthology of poems The Winnowing Fan. The work acts as a requiem for the dead of World War I and is dedicated "to the memory of our glorious men, with a special thought for the Worcesters".

works.title: The Starlight Express
works.wikipedia_extract: The Starlight Express is a children's play by Violet Pearn, based on the imaginative novel A Prisoner in Fairyland by Algernon Blackwood, with songs and incidental music written by the English composer Sir Edward Elgar in 1915.

works.title: The Sweepers
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Sweepers" is a poem written by Rudyard Kipling (1865-1936), and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1917, as the fourth of a set of four war-related songs on nautical subjects for which he chose the title "The Fringes of the Fleet".

works.title: The Torch
works.wikipedia_extract: The Torch is a song written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1909 as his Op.60, No.1.

works.title: The Wand of Youth
works.wikipedia_extract: The Wand of Youth Suites No. 1 and No. 2 are works for orchestra by Edward Elgar, first performed in 1907 and 1908 respectively. The titles Elgar gave them were, in full: The Wand of Youth First Suite, Op. 1a (1869–1907) and The Wand of Youth Second Suite,. The music was drawn from material written by the composer in his youth and orchestrated forty years or so later.

works.title: The Wind at Dawn
works.wikipedia_extract: "The Wind at Dawn" is a poem written by Caroline Alice Roberts, and set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1888.

works.title: There are seven that pull the thread
Three Bavarian Dances
works.wikipedia_extract: Three Bavarian Dances, Op. 27, is an orchestral work by Edward Elgar.

works.title: Une voix dans le désert
works.wikipedia_extract: Une voix dans le désert ("A Voice in the Desert") is a recitation, with a soprano soloist and orchestra, written by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1915 as his Op. 77. The French words are by the Belgian poet Émile Cammaerts.

works.title: Violin Concerto
works.wikipedia_extract: Edward Elgar's Violin Concerto in B minor, Op. 61, is one of his longest orchestral compositions, and the last of his works to gain immediate popular success.

works.title: Was it some Golden Star?
works.wikipedia_extract: Was it some Golden Star? is a poem written by Gilbert Parker, published in Volume I of a series of poems called Embers. It was set to music by the English composer Edward Elgar in 1910, as his Op. 59, No. 5.
